title: No-dig composting: using compost as a surface mulch
subtitle: Applying compost on top of the soil rather than digging it in
excerpt: The no-dig method applies compost to the soil surface rather than turning it in. Worms and soil organisms pull it down over the winter. Soil structure improves without disturbance. A 5 cm layer of compost applied in autumn or early spring is enough for most vegetable and perennial beds. Digging in compost is not necessary and in many cases makes soil structure worse.
sourceNotes: Charles Dowding no-dig method guides (nodiga.co.uk, 2023). Garden Organic no-dig research summaries. RHS guidance on mulching and soil improvement. Soil Association research on soil biology disturbance and tillage (2021).

glossaryTerms.definition: The web of fungal hyphae (fine threads) that connects plant roots and organic matter in healthy soil. Digging breaks up this network. No-dig methods preserve the fungal network by leaving the soil structure undisturbed. The network improves nutrient transfer and water retention.

content.text: Most UK gardening culture involves digging: turning over beds each autumn, breaking up soil, mixing in compost. The no-dig approach does none of this. Compost is laid on the surface. The soil beneath is never turned. Over several years, the soil structure improves because the
and worm channels are preserved. Weed germination also drops significantly because buried weed seeds are never brought to the surface.
When to apply compost as mulch
Autumn: the best time for most beds. Apply after clearing spent crops. The compost sits over winter while worms incorporate it. By spring, the top 5 cm of soil is noticeably looser and darker.
Early spring (February to March): before sowing or planting. Apply 3 to 5 cm, then sow or plant directly into the compost surface.
Mid-season: as a mulch around established plants to suppress weeds and hold moisture. Keep compost away from plant stems to avoid rot.
Vegetable beds: 5 cm per year. For a bed that has been no-dig for three or more years, 3 cm is enough.
Perennial borders: 5 cm every one to two years, applied in autumn or late winter.
Lawns (top-dressing): 1 to 2 cm in autumn, after scarifying. Work into the surface with a stiff broom.
Starting a new no-dig bed on grass or weeds: apply 10 to 20 cm of compost to smother existing growth. No need to remove the grass underneath.

Well-matured compost (6 months or more old) is best for direct sowing surfaces: its texture is fine and uniform. Fresh or part-finished compost works for autumn mulch where it will overwinter before planting. Lumpy compost with undigested material is fine for paths and deep no-dig bed bases.

No-dig does not eliminate weeds. It reduces them significantly in established beds. Perennial weeds (couch grass, bindweed, dock) present in the soil before starting no-dig may still emerge. These can be pulled when young; they weaken over time when denied light by the compost surface. In the first year of no-dig, expect more weed pressure than in subsequent years.
